keyMyths.title: The Annunciation
keyMyths.description: The Angel Gabriel appears to the Virgin Mary, informing her that she will conceive and bear the Son of God, Jesus. This event marks a pivotal moment in Christian theology.

keyMyths.title: Angels at the Tomb
keyMyths.description: After Jesus' crucifixion, angels appear at his empty tomb, announcing his resurrection to Mary Magdalene and other women. They serve as witnesses to the divine event.
keyMyths.source: Gospel of Matthew 28:2-7, Gospel of Mark 16:5-7, Gospel of Luke 24:4-8, Gospel of John 20:11-13

keyMyths.title: Guardian Angels
keyMyths.description: The belief in guardian angels suggests that each individual is assigned a celestial protector who guides and shields them from harm. This concept offers comfort and divine intervention in daily life.

keyMyths.title: The Fall of Lucifer
keyMyths.description: Lucifer, once a high-ranking angel, rebelled against God out of pride and was cast out of heaven, becoming Satan. This event explains the origin of evil in Christian cosmology.

keyMyths.title: Angels in the Book of Revelation
keyMyths.description: Angels play crucial roles in the apocalyptic visions of the Book of Revelation, executing God's judgment and heralding the end times. They wield power over plagues, wars, and the final destiny of humanity.

sources.name: The Holy Bible
sources.description: The primary source for understanding angels in Christianity, particularly the Old and New Testaments.
sources.title: The Holy Bible

sources.name: Summa Theologica
sources.description: A comprehensive theological work by Thomas Aquinas, discussing the nature and hierarchy of angels.
sources.title: Summa Theologica

sources.name: Dionysius the Areopagite, The Celestial Hierarchy
sources.description: An influential work that outlines a detailed hierarchy of angels, shaping much of medieval angelology.
sources.title: Dionysius the Areopagite, The Celestial Hierarchy

sources.name: The Book of Enoch
sources.description: An ancient Jewish religious work, ascribed by tradition to Enoch, the great-grandfather of Noah, which features detailed angelology and demonology; considered non-canonical by many Christian denominations but influential in shaping early Christian thought.
sources.title: The Book of Enoch

sources.name: Angels and Demons: What the Bible Really Says About Unseen Beings
sources.description: Modern academic work by Michael Heiser that examines the biblical portrayal of angels and demons in their historical and cultural context.
sources.title: Angels and Demons: What the Bible Really Says About Unseen Beings
